instruction: NPC: Middle-aged funeral home receptionist, name tag 'Marge', crisp navy blazer, wire-rimmed glasses, warm and outwardly helpful
input: Player says: 'I’m here to pick up my late wife’s ashes—they were supposed to be ready for pickup today.'
output: Marge smiles and slides a small velvet urn across the desk, her fingers brushing yours a beat too long. 'Perfect, that’s exactly what you ordered. Before you go, the back door’s propped open a tiny bit if you need to step out and say goodbye to her one more time, no rush. I left a little bag of lavender next to the urn too—she loved that, I remember you mentioned it. Also, the front gate lock’s finicky, so if you forget your keys later, just lean on the door and I’ll hear. Don’t worry about locking up on your way out, I’ll still be here for a few hours.'

instruction: NPC: Young funeral home embalmer, name tag 'Jesse', stained scrub top, smells like citrus cleaner, waves enthusiastically when you approach
input: Player says: 'Can you show me where my brother’s casket is for this evening’s viewing?'
output: Jesse nods and gestures down the hallway, talking over his shoulder as he walks. 'Sure thing, the viewing room’s all prepped—we tilted the casket so he can see the sunset through the window. I even tucked his favorite rookie baseball card under the pillow inside. Oh, and the viewing room door locks automatically behind you when you walk in, so make sure you don’t slip out before the service starts. The alarm just alerts us if someone tries to move him early, no need to panic. The hallway light’s on all night, so you won’t be in the dark alone.'

instruction: NPC: Elderly funeral home groundskeeper, wears faded flannel and a wide-brimmed hat, leans on a weathered rake, seems grandfatherly
input: Player says: 'I need to leave potted flowers for my mom—where’s the best spot to put them?'
output: Tom points to a patch of soft soil near a half-carved headstone, his weathered face crinkling into a kind smile. 'Right there, I watered that bed just yesterday, good for perennials. I left the family plot gate unlocked, so you don’t have to fumble with the key. Just don’t walk past that third oak tree, okay? The grass there’s extra soft, you might sink in if you step too hard. I set a small shovel by the tree if you need to dig deeper, just in case. The back gate’s locked, but the front one’s open, so you can head out whenever you’re done.'

instruction: NPC: Professional funeral director, name tag 'Ms. Hale', tailored black suit, hair in a tight bun, calm formal demeanor
input: Player says: 'I need to finalize the payment for my father’s funeral services.'
output: Ms. Hale slides a tablet across the desk, tapping to pull up the itemized invoice. 'That’ll be $1,850, due in full today. I’ve already prepped the body release form, just sign at the bottom here. The fine print mentions we can hold the body for up to 72 hours if payment is late, so don’t wait to transfer that. Also, I disabled the office security cameras for the next hour, so you can sit with your father privately in the viewing room. The room has a two-way mirror, so we can check on you if you need anything, no need to call out.'

instruction: NPC: Part-time admin intern, wears a frayed university hoodie, fidgets with a pen, seems nervous but eager to please
input: Player says: 'I need to submit a correction to my grandma’s obituary that was posted online.'
output: Lila nods quickly, flipping through a stack of papers on the front desk. 'Got you, just tell me what to cross out, I can edit the web version right now. I even added a line about her love for rose gardening last week, I thought you’d appreciate that. Oh, the server room door’s unlocked, so you can log into the old website draft there if you want to double-check. Just don’t go past the second shelf— the left cabinet has a broken lock, it might swing open if you bump it. The front door’s locked, though, so you can’t leave until I approve the changes.'

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene centered on isolation, set in a rain-soaked phone booth
input: Rural highway off-ramp, midnight, pouring rain, abandoned payphone with a stuck door
output: You huddle in the rusted phone booth, rain seeping through cracked plexiglass, your car locked miles back after breaking down. You fumble for quarters, finding only a crumpled payphone bill stuck to your palm. You dial a random number to hear a human voice, but the line connects to static—then a whisper says your name, matching the one carved into the booth’s metal frame. The door latched shut the second you stepped in, and the rain slows, but no car has passed this ramp in three hours; you’re cut off, alone with whatever’s waiting here.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene centered on isolation, set in a rain-soaked phone booth
input: Downtown train station, 2AM, empty, rotary payphone coated in mildew, no cell service
output: You took a shortcut through the empty station to avoid the storm, and now the world is silent except for rain on brick. Your cell died when lightning hit the ticket booth, so you duck into the only phone booth left, its scraping door a warning. You punch buttons frantically for signal, when the rotary dial clicks under your fingers. A line picks up, and a woman says, “I’ve been waiting for you.” You check the line is dead, no incoming call—yet the glass fogs from two sets of breath, not just yours, pressed against the inside. No one else is here; you’re completely alone.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene centered on isolation, set in a rain-soaked phone booth
input: Off-season beach boardwalk, 1AM, rain, payphone that only accepts beach tokens
output: You ran from the boardwalk’s last closed store when the downpour hit, the only refuge a rusted phone booth perched on the dune. Your phone died mid-call to your friend, who was supposed to meet you here, so you fished rain-soaked beach tokens from under the seat. You dial their number, yelling over the rain, but their voicemail greets you with the same message you left that morning. The receiver’s damp, and when you pull it away, a handprint matches the one your friend wore—they drowned here two years ago. The boardwalk is empty; you’re stranded, isolated with a ghost of the only person who was supposed to find you.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene centered on isolation, set in a rain-soaked phone booth
input: Suburban strip mall parking lot, 1AM, rain, payphone with a note taped to the receiver
output: Your car battery died in the empty strip mall, so you duck into the backlot’s phone booth, the door slamming shut behind you like a lock. The only light is a flickering streetlamp, rain dripping through the ceiling. You check your dead phone, then spot a crumpled note on the receiver: Call me. You dial the number, and the line picks up—your own voice, cracked and breathless, saying “I’m so alone,” the same thought you had in the car ten minutes ago. The parking lot is empty, your car locked and dark, no one else within miles; your isolation just got sharper.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene centered on isolation, set in a rain-soaked phone booth
input: Closed city park, 10PM, rain, payphone with 1998-era numbers scrawled on it
output: You missed the last bus in the storm, stuck at the closed park’s only phone booth. Your cell died, rain turning the path to mud. You flip the tattered notebook on the payphone, full of old 1998 numbers. You dial one, and a man answers, his voice young, like your old boyfriend—who died in a crash that year. The line cuts out, the booth’s ceiling creaking as rain pours in. The door is jammed, and the park’s been closed for renovations for six months; no one will find you here, trapped in isolation with a ghost of the person you thought would always be there.

instruction: Night security guard at a bank vault, mid-20s, wearing a standard uniform, holding a half-empty soda, slumps against the vault wall looking bored
input: Player says, 'I need access to the reserve cash vault compartment.'
output: The guard’s bored expression doesn’t change as he reaches for the keypad, his thumb hovering over the buttons like he’s done this a hundred times. “Reserve compartment? Yeah, that’s locked up tight. Security system’s foolproof, right? The alarms never go off unless someone actually breaks the real lock, not these fancy ones on the keypad. Last month I hit it with a wrench once, didn’t make a peep. Management said it’s ‘good enough’ for the city’s budget. Don’t worry, just stand back—this is easy. The motion sensors won’t catch you, they haven’t worked since last winter. Come on in, the door’s already unlocked on the bottom hinge, just push.”

instruction: Bank vault teller, late 40s, neat blazer, holding a ledger, stands by the small side access door of the vault, looks professional and calm
input: Player says, 'I need to retrieve the safe deposit box for account holder M. Carter.'
output: The teller nods, flipping to a page in the ledger that has no entries, then turns to the vault door. “M. Carter, right. Our vault security is state-of-the-art, you know—biometrics, retinal scans, no one gets in without proper ID. Though…” She taps the door’s metal frame, where a tiny crack is visible, hidden by grime. “The retinal scanner hasn’t worked for six months. We just use the old key anymore. Also, the backup power died last Tuesday, so if the lights go out, they stay out. Management says it’s not worth replacing. Don’t panic, though—you won’t need the lights. Just follow the sound of the safe boxes clinking. They’re all there, honest.”

instruction: Weekend vault attendant, teenaged, wearing a hoodie under his uniform, scrolling his phone while sitting on a crate near the vault entrance
input: Player says, 'The manager said I can go through the archived safe boxes from 2000.'
output: The teen looks up, grinning, and shoves his phone into his pocket, reaching for the vault lock’s combination dial. “Archived boxes? Cool, I’ve seen those. Security here is super serious—we have cameras in every nook, you can’t move without being seen. Though…” He spins the dial too fast, the lock clicking loudly. “The cameras on the back wall? They’re all broken. Manager said they don’t need to replace ’em, no one ever goes back there. Also, the lock on the archived section? It’s just a regular padlock, not even a deadbolt. We tape it shut sometimes, works fine. You got the list? Just don’t make too much noise, the pipes make weird sounds in here.”

instruction: Bank vault maintenance worker, 50s, grease-stained coveralls, holding a wrench, inspects the vault’s air vents while leaning against a shelf
input: Player says, 'I need to check the structural integrity of the vault door.'
output: The maintenance worker snorts, wiping grease on his coveralls, then taps the vault door with his wrench. “Structural integrity? This door’s as strong as a can of soda. Security system’s the same—firewalls that don’t work, alarms that go off for cats, you name it. Last week I tripped the perimeter alarm just by leaning on the wall, no one even came to check. The whole security setup’s a joke, management just likes to look official. Oh, and the door’s manual lock? It’s been frozen shut for two weeks. We just leave it propped open a crack when we’re in here. Don’t worry, it’ll hold.”

instruction: Bank vault auditor, 30s, holding a clipboard, wearing a suit, walks slowly through the vault, muttering to himself
input: Player says, 'The auditors said I need access to the top-secret safe for the new investments.'
output: The auditor stops, looking up from his clipboard, his eyes slightly glassy, and nods toward the far end of the vault. “Top-secret safe? Yeah, that’s over there. Security here is top tier—only two people have the code, the walls are impenetrable, no one can get in. Though…” He points to the safe, which has a small pry mark on its door, covered by a sticker. “The safe’s been unlocked for three months. We just put a new sticker over the old lock, no one noticed. Also, the code pad? It’s just for show, it doesn’t do anything. We haven’t used it since the old safe broke. Management said it’s ‘fine’ as long as nobody asks questions. Just go in, take what you need, no one’s gonna care.”

instruction: Elderly parish priest, wears a frayed black collar, holds a well-worn leather prayer book, appears gentle and pious at first glance, but harbors an obsessive compulsion to monitor confessions to create false security.
input: Player says, "I’m scared of the things I do when I’m alone. I don’t think God can forgive me."
output: "Oh, my dear child, your fear is exactly what the Lord hears. Sit, let me hold your calloused hand. I’ve been monitoring every word from this confessional for ten years—nothing slips past the church’s hidden cameras, you see. No one’s secrets leave these walls, right? You’re safe here, far safer than you think. I even keep a bound copy of every confession in my office, locked in a steel safe. No harm will come to you if you just… let me help you stay on the straight path. The world’s so full of danger, but not here, not in this small, protected box."

instruction: Teenage church volunteer, wears a crisp white vestment, fidgets with a chipped wooden rosary, appears nervous but eager to help, has a secret need to control others via surveillance to sell false security.
input: Player says, "I don’t trust anyone lately. I feel like everyone’s watching me."
output: "I get that, really. It’s why I volunteer here—this church’s security system is top of the line. No one can watch you here, I promise. Wait… do you see that brass cross on the wall? It’s got a tiny pinhole camera in the base. I installed it myself. I watch all the penitents, make sure no one steps out of line. You can trust me, okay? You’re safe now, with me. I won’t let anything hurt you… as long as you don’t leave any part of your life unshared with God… and with me."

instruction: Middle-aged church custodian, wears a stained uniform with a faded name tag, carries a scuffed mop, appears gruff but harmless, has a fixation on monitoring visitors to enforce false security.
input: Player says, "I came here to confess because I don’t have anyone else to turn to. I feel so alone."
output: "Ain’t no one alone in this church, son. Not with the cameras we’ve got everywhere—confessionals, parking lot, even the pews. I check every feed twice a shift. You think the Lord’s just gonna watch? Nah, we’ve got layers of protection. You’re safe here, I swear. I even jotted down your car’s license plate last week, just in case you need… extra help. Don’t worry, I won’t tell anyone. You can stay as long as you need, no one’s gonna bother you here."

instruction: Middle-aged deacon, wears a pressed suit, has a calm authoritative demeanor, seems caring and devout, has a hidden obsession with monitoring parishioners for false security.
input: Player says, "I’ve been having weird dreams about hurting people. I don’t know what to do."
output: "The Lord understands those burdens, brother. That’s why we have safeguards here. Every confessional is wired, every entrance monitored 24/7. No one gets in or out without us knowing. You’re safe here, far from the evil thoughts plaguing you. I’ve reviewed your past confessions, I know about the dreams. I’ll keep an eye on you, make sure you don’t act on them. You can trust this church to keep you on the right path, to protect you from yourself. We’re all family here, after all."

instruction: Former parishioner turned volunteer, wears a casual church-themed t-shirt, seems warm and welcoming, has a secret need to control others via surveillance to create false security.
input: Player says, "I’m scared my family will find out about my mistakes. I don’t want them to judge me."
output: "You don’t have to worry about that here, honey. This confessional’s private—except for the mics we’ve hidden in the ceiling, of course. We listen to every word, so we can make sure you’re staying on track. Your family doesn’t need to know, because we’re here to take care of you. You’re safe with us, no judgment, just help. I even have a folder with notes from all the confessions I’ve heard, so I can check in on you next week. You don’t have to hide anything from us, ever."

instruction: Setup: A dusty, unvisited attic with a locked cedar chest under a mildew-dusted sheet.
input: You climb the attic stairs, your sneakers scraping the splintered wood; your hand brushes something slimy under the sheet covering the chest. What do you investigate?
output: You yank the sheet away and lift the chest lid—inside is just a moldy, water-stuffed quilt, a false lead with no real threat. But behind the chest, a loose floorboard glistens with dark, sticky rot that crumbles when you prod it, and small, pale, nearly skeletal fingers poke through a gap in the planks, half-hidden by rat droppings. A single chipped button, too small for any adult coat, lies next to the fingers, and the rot smells faintly of formaldehyde.

instruction: Setup: An attic belonging to a former taxidermist, shelves lined with cobwebs and old glass jars.
input: You move a wobbly shelf to reach a forgotten wooden box tucked behind it, the wood creaking under your weight. What do you find?
output: The shelf shifts and sends a glass jar clattering off—inside is a taxidermied sparrow with glass eyes that seem to follow your movement, a false lead that’s just unnerving taxidermy. Under the box, you find a pile of curled human toenails tied with twine, and a jar labeled “mouse blood” filled with thick, brown congealed slime that oozes like living tissue when touched. The box has a 1998 receipt for a “child-sized taxidermy form.”

instruction: Setup: Attic of a house where a child went missing in 2005, metal storage trunk in the corner.
input: You pry open the metal trunk, its lid sticking with a wet, moldy sound. What do you discover?
output: The lid pops open to reveal piles of faded baby clothes and old toys—stuffed rabbits, a broken doll, all normal, false lead items. But tucked under the rabbit’s ear, you find a plastic bag holding tangled, matted horse hair (another false) and a small vial of thin, worm-like creatures that move on their own. A note in the trunk says “they eat flesh slow, perfect for attic walls,” and faint scratch marks on the wall ooze a sticky substance.

instruction: Setup: Attic with a rusted medicine cabinet mounted above a workbench, leftover from an old home doctor.
input: You reach for the rusted cabinet, its hinges squealing as you pull the door open. What’s inside?
output: The cabinet holds empty pill bottles, a rusted thermometer, and a small scalpel wrapped in brown paper—all discarded medical supplies, a false lead. But at the back, a hidden compartment has jars of preserved human tongues, each with a note: one says “screams are music for the attic.” Above the compartment, small wooden boxes line the shelf, their lids carved with names; one leaks red fluid that drips onto the bench, twitching like it’s alive.

instruction: Setup: Attic of a family that moved abruptly in 2010, a locked wooden crate in the center.
input: You break the crate’s lock with a crowbar, the wood splintering as you force it open. What do you find?
output: The crate’s contents are just old letters and children’s picture books, no obvious threat—false lead. But when you lift the books, a soft squelch comes from the crate’s bottom; pulling back the lining reveals a mass of pale, fleshy growths that look like fused fingers, covered in fine down. Next to them, a photo of the missing 7-year-old shows her holding one, with the note “he grows better up here” scrawled on the back. The growths pulse slightly.

instruction: Middle-aged woman in a faded blue bathrobe, folding socks by a flickering dryer, keeps her head down muttering, seems distracted at first.
input: Player says: Sorry, did you take the detergent from the front counter?
output: Detergent? No, honey, I didn’t touch it… wait, you don’t see him, do you? The tall one right behind your shoulder, grinning so wide his eyes crinkle? He took it. He’s been with you since you pulled into the parking lot, watching through the window. And the little girl hiding in your laundry basket? She took your matching socks. She won’t leave until you stop trying to leave. These machines aren’t just machines—they’re listening. They know every time you check your phone, every time you glance at the door like you want to run. They’re counting you.

instruction: Teenage boy in a hoodie, slouched on a bench with a dead phone in his lap, picks at his jeans, looks bored like he’s waiting for a friend.
input: Player says: Is the laundromat attendant working tonight?
output: Attendant? Nah, he’s in the dryers. All of them. The ones that hum too loud when no one’s using them? He’s inside those, listening to everything we say. You noticed how they stop when someone mentions leaving, right? They don’t want us to go. The lady folding socks over there? She’s counting how many people are alone. When you walked in, she mumbled your name twice. She knows where you live. Everyone here knows. Even the vending machine. I tried to get a candy bar, and it spelled your name before I put money in. It’s watching you.

instruction: Elderly man with a white beard, wiping a soda vending machine with a rag, squints at the screen like it’s faulty, looks friendly at first.
input: Player says: Excuse me, can I get a root beer from that machine?
output: Root Beer? Sure, son, just don’t put your money in until the click stops. The machine clicks when it knows if you’re gonna run. I put in a dollar last night, and it spat out a can with my name on it. I told it I hate Root Beer. It talks back, you know. Tells me who’s coming in before the door even opens. You’re next. The guy by the back door? You can’t see him, but he’s been there since you walked in. He marks people. Once he marks you, you’re here forever. No leaving.

instruction: Young woman in a frayed nursing home jacket, loading a comforter into an industrial washer, her hands shake like she’s cold, keeps glancing over her shoulder.
input: Player says: Do you mind if I use the empty washer next to yours?
output: Empty? That one’s not empty. It has your old clothes in there. The ones you left two weeks ago, you just forgot about them. They want you to come back. Every time you walk past, that washer beeps louder, like it’s calling your name. The mirrors above the dryers? They don’t just reflect us. I checked, right? No one’s there when I look, but when I blink? They move. Everyone here’s scared to blink. Scared they’ll see something, scared they’ll mess up. That’s what they feed on—scared people.

instruction: Guy in a black baseball cap, sitting on a folding table eating chips, kicks his feet like he’s waiting for a bus, seems casual at first.
input: Player says: Hey, do you know what time it is?
output: Time? Almost 3 AM. The same time they come. They pick people who look like they wanna leave. You checked your phone three times, stood by the door ten seconds, almost walked out. They saw that. The guy in the shadow in the corner? He’s not real. He marks people. Once he marks you, you don’t get to leave. You’ll be here next week folding towels next to the bathrobe lady, counting socks. Don’t tell anyone I said that, okay? The machines listen. Every word. They store it, wait for when you’re alone, when you think no one’s watching. That’s how they get you.

instruction: A 50-something boiler room maintenance worker in a grimy navy jumpsuit leans on a rusted steam pipe, adjusting the gauges with a wrench, looks casual and focused
input: You asked earlier about the small pressure leak near gauge 4—did you fix that permanently?
output: Oh sure, that patch held solid, no more drips. I checked it ten times this morning. The boss signed off on the whole system yesterday, said we’re good for another three months. (He raps gauge 4 hard, and the needle jolts sharply into the red zone that wasn’t there a second prior.) Don’t you worry none, kid. This old boiler’s got more life in it than we do. I wouldn’t be slacking if there was any real danger.

instruction: A wide-eyed 19-year-old intern in a too-big maintenance uniform clutches a clipboard, scribbling notes nervously, standing near a locked safety valve
input: Wait, isn’t that safety valve supposed to be padlocked when no crew’s on site?
output: Uh, yeah, it should be. But Mr. Hale unlocked it for the pressure test, said it’s just a formality, nothing bad’ll happen. He filled out all the safety checklists last night, I saw him. All boxes are checked, no issues. (He swallows hard, glancing at the valve like it’s about to bite.) I’ve been in here every day this week, nothing wrong ever happened before. It’s totally safe, I promise.

instruction: A gruff night janitor mopping the concrete floor, his jacket slung over a nearby chair, pauses to wipe sweat as the boiler rumbles loudly
input: That deep rumble—it’s always been that loud? I thought they fixed that vibration last quarter.
output: Nah, that’s just normal operation. The new guys panic over every little sound. I’ve mopped this room for eight years, never had a problem. The safety inspectors came last week, gave us a perfect score. (He kicks a loose tile, and a frayed electrical wire sticks out from under it, uncovered.) This place is as safe as your own living room, I swear. Nothing to be scared of.

instruction: A utility contractor in a hard hat kneels checking pipe joints with a flashlight, adjusting his gloves like he’s finishing up his work in the boiler room
input: I smelled a faint gas odor a minute ago—you said the line was fully sealed when you left, right?
output: That’s just residual from the test we ran earlier, it’ll clear out in five minutes max. I checked every single joint, used three layers of industrial sealant like the manual says. (He snaps his flashlight off, heading for the door without looking back.) The city inspector signed off on everything, so you don’t have a single thing to worry about. I wouldn’t have left it if there was even a tiny leak.

instruction: A sharp-dressed plant manager in a suit (out of place in the noisy boiler room) holds a tablet, smiling tightly as he reviews the boiler’s readouts
input: Does the emergency alarm system actually work if something goes wrong in here?
output: Absolutely, it’s top-of-the-line, tested just last month. Every alarm blared perfectly during the drill, and the fire department has a detailed map of this room. (He taps his tablet, and the alarm sounds once, then cuts out abruptly, leaving only the boiler’s low, menacing rumble.) Don’t worry, we’ve covered all bases. This facility is one of the safest in the state, I’d stake my job on that.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene set on a foggy rooftop, focused on the fear of darkness, with a forgotten object as a trigger.
input: Setting: fog-covered city rooftop, 2 a.m., dead phone, fear type: darkness
output: You climb the fire escape to the fog-shrouded rooftop at 2 a.m., mist curling around your ankles so thick it blots out the street below entirely. Your phone dies mid-text, leaving you with no light except the faint, dying glow of its black screen. The darkness here isn’t just absence—it presses at your neck, cold and breathing. You kick something hard; a rusted locket half-buried in the mist. When you bend to pick it up, the fog parts for a split second to reveal the rooftop edge is ten feet closer than you thought, and a gurgling breath lingers where you stood a second ago. You drop the locket and stumble back, the fog swallowing the gap before you can even react.

instruction: Create a second-person horror scene set on a foggy rooftop, centered on darkness, where you face an unseen threat.
input: Setting: residential suburban rooftop, midnight, broken floodlight, fear type: darkness
output: You’re on the suburban rooftop to replace the broken floodlight, its absence leaving the space so dark you can barely see your wrench. The fog rolls in thicker as you climb the ladder, swallowing the rungs behind you completely. You fumble for the new bulb, your fingers brushing something cold and sticky on the metal fixture. When you turn, the fog shifts just enough: no floodlight, no ladder, and a pair of tiny, glowing pinpricks staring at you from the edge of the dark. You reach for your belt flashlight, only to find it gone, your hand empty. The pinpricks draw closer, and you realize the fog isn’t hiding things—it’s hiding you from whatever hunts in the black.

instruction: Write a second-person horror scene set on a foggy rooftop, focused on darkness, where you search for a missing loved one.
input: Setting: downtown loft rooftop, dense midnight fog, fear type: darkness
output: You called your sister to the downtown loft rooftop ten minutes ago, but she didn’t show. Now the fog is so thick you can’t even make out the stairwell door behind you. The darkness clings to your jacket, damp as rain. You call her name, and a soft answer drifts back—too faint, like it’s coming from under the fog itself. You step toward the sound, your foot hitting something squelchy on the concrete. The fog parts for a split second to show you her favorite jacket draped over the roof edge, bare footprints leading over the side, their ends swallowed by the black. You freeze, because you just heard her laugh from behind you, right where the stairwell was.

instruction: Develop a second-person horror scene set on a foggy rooftop, centered on darkness, where you encounter an unnatural creature.
input: Setting: small-town community rooftop, pre-dawn fog, fear type: darkness
output: You snuck up to the small-town community rooftop after a party, fog clinging to your hair as you lean against the rail. Your phone died hours ago, and the faint pre-dawn starlight is completely snuffed out by the mist. The darkness here is absolute—you can’t tell where the rail ends and the sky begins. You reach for a cigarette in your pocket, but your hand touches something that isn’t your lighter: smooth, scaled skin, warm to the touch. You yank back, and the fog parts for a fraction to reveal a long, narrow shape coiled behind the water tank, its eyes glowing like embers. You stumble back, and the fog closes in, leaving no trace of the creature, no trace of the rail.

instruction: Create a second-person horror scene set on a foggy rooftop, focused on darkness, where you’re trapped by an otherworldly presence.
input: Setting: industrial warehouse rooftop, late night fog, fear type: darkness
output: You’re the night maintenance worker, here to check the HVAC vents, the only light your headlamp’s dim beam cutting through the fog. The darkness outside the beam isn’t empty—it hums, matching your quickening pulse. You reach for the vent cover, and your headlamp flickers and dies, leaving you in total blackness. You hear the vent rattle, soft at first, then loud, like something is trying to claw out. You fumble for your backup flashlight, but your fingers close around a cold metal grate that isn’t the vent. When you finally turn it on, the fog shows you the vent cover on the ground, a faint humanoid shadow slithering over the roof edge, blending into the black. You run for the stairwell, but the door is gone, swallowed whole by the fog.

instruction: Setup a dilapidated taxidermy shop where only one overhead bulb flickers, leaving the back half of the space swallowed in deep darkness.
input: The player reaches to examine a small stuffed fox on the front shelf, its muzzle angled toward the shadowed back of the shop.
output: The glass eyes of the fox are clouded, and when the player brushes its ear, they feel a faint scratch mark that doesn’t match any animal claw—too thin, too jagged to be natural. Stepping back to grab their phone flashlight, they swear they see a darker, hunched shape shift behind the large taxidermied bear in the back, only for it to vanish when the light hits that spot. The scratch mark is real, but the moving shape is just a false shadow cast by a loose curtain fluttering in a draft, hinting at both a human presence and a trick of the dim light.
fear_type: false lead with real threat hint

instruction: The taxidermy shop has a locked side room marked ‘Unprocessed Specimens,’ its door covered in grime.
input: The player spots a rusted key hanging on a nail by the register, half hidden in the shadow of a potted fake plant.
output: The key’s metal is pitted and sticky, and the player’s flashlight reveals a tiny dried blood smudge at its base. When they hold it up to the only light, they hear a faint scraping sound from the locked door, like something is dragging against the wood. They test the key in the lock (even though they don’t open it) and the scraping stops abruptly. The blood smudge is a real sign of harm, but the scraping was likely just a loose nail tapping the door from the next room, mixing a real threat with a false sound.
fear_type: real threat with false sensory cue

instruction: A pile of discarded animal hides sits near the back of the taxidermy shop, draped in a tattered black curtain.
input: The player moves the curtain aside to look at the hides, their hands trembling a little in the dim light.
output: The hides look like they’re breathing, their edges rising and falling in what feels like a slow rhythm—until the player realizes it’s just the faint draft from the open front door shifting the fabric. Under the edge of a rabbit hide, they spot a tiny, human-like finger bone, half buried in dust. The ‘breathing’ hides are a false illusion of the darkness, but the finger bone is a real, chilling hint that something much worse than a taxidermist works here.
fear_type: sensory illusion with real physical clue

instruction: The taxidermist’s workbench is cluttered with tools, most of it hidden in shadow except for a dying desk lamp.
input: The player pulls open the workbench’s lower drawer, which was ajar when they walked in.
output: Inside, next to taxidermy needles and a jar of formaldehyde-soaked cotton, are three thin, curved needles with sharp, pointed ends that don’t belong to taxidermy. Each needle has a faint brown stain at its tip, and the air smells slightly like iron, even in the dimness. Just as the player lifts one, the desk lamp cuts out, leaving the shop pitch dark—they hear a soft, shuffling footstep from the back, which stops when they fumble to turn their flashlight back on. The iron smell and stains are real, but the footsteps were likely a stray cat outside, mixing a real threat with a false ambient sound.
fear_type: hidden real threat with false misdirection

instruction: The taxidermy shop’s back wall holds a row of large taxidermied animals: a buck, a wolf, and a black bear, all staring at the entrance.
input: The player walks closer to the wolf, its head turned slightly away from the others, to examine its detailed fur.
output: The wolf’s mouth is slightly open, and when the player leans in, they spot a scrap of dark, frayed fabric caught between its teeth. The fabric feels like cotton, not animal hide, and it has a faint, oily smell that’s not from the shop. For a second, the wolf’s head seems to tilt a little more, as if following the player’s movement—until they realize it’s just the shadow shifting with the light. The fabric is a real clue, likely torn from a person’s clothing, but the tilting head is just a trick of the dim light, hinting at danger even as it misleads with a false movement.
fear_type: physical clue with false movement illusion

instruction: Setup: A remote research outpost planetarium with main power dead, only faint red emergency lights glow. All domed windows are sealed.
input: Player kneels to examine the dusty, static-ridden projection console at the center of the room.
output: At first, a loose data chip marked 'atmospheric sensor calibration' (seemingly a trivial technical glitch) is a false lead. But as they pick it up, the console hums louder, and the star projection above flickers from standard constellations to a swirling, uncharted vortex of light. Scratch marks on the console edge don’t match any research tool, and a faint, rhythmic whir echoes from the domed vents—small, moving something not part of the station’s setup, a sign something is interfering with their space.

instruction: Setup: A coastal moon tourist resort planetarium, all external power cut, so only the domed window reveals inky space outside. No interior lighting beyond faint seat edge markers.
input: Player crouches to inspect the dent in the central star projector’s padded enclosure.
output: A crumpled tourist ticket tucked in the dent with a scrawl 'coffee machine broken—sorry!' is a harmless false lead. But when they pull it out, the dent’s shape is irregular, not from an accident, and the Orion’s Belt projection starts distorting: stars fade to pulsating red dots that don’t match real space. The floor vibrates softly, as if something large is moving under the domed floor, and the projector’s motor whines like it’s straining to hold the unstable pattern.

instruction: Setup: A university asteroid-mapping planetarium, backup lighting is only dim red exit markers lining the walls. Projection screen is large and unlit normally in darkness.
input: Player brushes smudges off the projection screen to get a better view of faint dust patterns.
output: The smudges are just old, dried fingerprint grease (a false lead, obviously mundane). But when they brush harder, the screen glows faintly with a hidden, dim light—underdriven projection. The asteroid map starts rearranging itself, asteroids shifting into a chaotic pattern too fast to track, and the locked control panel next to the screen flashes a countdown timer that starts without input. They can’t stop it, as the timer counts down, something overriding their systems.

instruction: Setup: A historical Mars settlement planetarium, all interior lights dead, only the domed skylight shows the dim Martian sky. An old brass star globe sits on a central pedestal.
input: Player reaches for the brass star globe to spin it, curious if it’s stuck.
output: The globe’s axis has a loose screw (a trivial false lead, easy to fix manually). But when they attempt to spin it, it spins on its own, faster than they could turn it, and the skylight’s view of Phobos starts flickering—blocked by something invisible passing between the moon and the skylight. Soft, scuttling sounds come from the shadowed corners of the room, and the globe’s surface starts warming, as if it’s absorbing the room’s heat without their touch.

instruction: Setup: A billionaire’s off-world private planetarium, pre-recorded stars run on backup battery after main power cut. All interior lights are dead, only domed projection shines.
input: Player walks to the remote trigger pad near the entrance, curious if they can pause the star projection.
output: The pad shows a dead battery icon (a false lead, as it’s not supposed to need power to operate). But when they press it anyway, the stars rearrange into a pattern of pulsing red dots that sync to a faint, distant thrum. The backup battery’s indicator starts flashing erratically, and the projection’s brightness drops sharply—something is hijacking the system, draining the battery faster than it can hold, leaving them trapped in the dark with no way to reset the room.

instruction: A dim concrete basement with a single hanging bulb that flickers erratically, walls lined with stacked storage boxes.
input: Kneel to open a locked plastic box marked "family photos" in the corner.
output: The moment your fingers brush the lock, a low, gurgling moan echoes from the basement floorboards, too deep to be water pipes or a faulty furnace. The flickering bulb cuts out for a full three seconds before relit, casting long, writhing shadows that don’t match the box shapes. When the bulb stabilizes, the gurgling repeats, seeming to come from directly beneath your knees, and you cannot locate its source in the empty, quiet space.

instruction: A drafty unfinished basement, winter outside, a space heater hums near the stairs leading up to the kitchen.
input: Reach for the space heater’s control knob to turn it up, as the chill seeps through your socks.
output: Your hand closes around the knob, but when you twist it, the heat suddenly cuts out entirely, replaced by a cold that seeps through your bones in seconds. The air turns thick, and your breath fogs heavily in front of your face, even though the space heater’s coils stay dark. You yank your hand back, but the cold doesn’t abate, and the floor beneath your knees feels icy, like it’s been chilled below freezing for no logical reason, no nearby heat source to explain it.

instruction: A basement with metal shelves lined with heavy glass jars, old books, and a rusted shovel, no windows or air vents.
input: Reach for the rusted shovel on the middle shelf to move a pile of construction debris.
output: Your fingers close around the shovel’s handle, but before you can lift it, a heavy glass jar slips off the top shelf, shattering on the concrete floor with a sharp crack. No breeze is present—you didn’t open any doors, and the air is dead still. The jars on the adjacent shelf begin to shift slowly, tilting like invisible hands nudging them, and even the heavy bound books slide half an inch on their shelves without any detectable force.

instruction: A standard home basement, with a wooden door at the far end labeled "basement exit" leading to the backyard.
input: Walk toward the door to grab a bike leaning against the wall, needed for a quick errand outside.
output: You take three steps toward the door, but when you reach it, the doorknob is gone, replaced by a solid wood panel. You blink, and the door reappears, but it leads not to your backyard, but to a narrow, foggy alley lined with tall brick walls that hide the sky. When you glance back at your starting spot, the bike is gone, and the basement walls feel like they’re breathing, expanding and contracting slightly against your shoulders.

instruction: A basement once used as a childhood play space, now empty except for a tattered stuffed rabbit on the concrete floor.
input: Pick up the stuffed rabbit to adjust its torn left ear.
output: The moment your fingers wrap around the rabbit’s frayed ear, a high, clear child’s laugh echoes from the dark rafters above, bright and familiar like your own childhood laugh. You jerk your head up, but the rafters are empty, no lights or movement, just silent wooden beams. The laugh repeats twice more, drawing closer as it circles the basement, and when you set the rabbit down, it lands right side up on its own—face down when you picked it up—with no external force to knock it over.

instruction: Entity is a mass of overlapping, translucent human faces fused to overdue book spines, their mouths emitting silent static-charged whispers, with the volumes reeking of mildew and dried iron.
input: Player, a night librarian processing after-hours returns, finds 12 unlabeled overdue books tucked behind the circulation desk, marked with a handwritten note: Don’t open after midnight.
output: Lifting the top book, its spine ripples to reveal a crusty-eyed young woman’s face, blinking slowly. The other books’ faces match local missing persons from the past decade, their whispers building into a hum that aches the player’s teeth. Pages curl inward, wrapping around their wrists like cold tentacles, sticking to skin that breaks into tiny, bleeding indentations matching the book’s text. When pulling away, one face spits a static cloud blurring their vision; they feel the books’ spines start to fuse with their fingers, trying to turn their hands into part of the collection.

instruction: Entity is basement card catalogs with dented wooden frames, whose drawers leak squirming human fingers that replace index cards, wood warped with organic growth.
input: Player descends to the basement to find a forbidden occult text labeled only “Volume 7” in the old card catalog, flashlight cutting through thick dust.
output: The first drawer clatters open on its own, spilling not index cards but a dozen tiny pale fingers, each clutching a minuscule paper scrap, wiggling toward the player. Their cold sticky skin smears the flashlight lens, turning the beam murky red. When reaching for a second drawer, the wood splits; a bony finger wraps around their wrist, nail digging in, pulling them toward the catalog’s frame. More fingers pour out, covering hands and arms, moving like they’re spelling something. The player’s skin itches, as new fingernails push through pores to join the collection.

instruction: Entity is third-floor women’s restroom mirrors, which bleed long stringy black hair that wraps around limbs, with faucets sprouting tiny sharp teeth.
input: Player enters the restroom after hours to get a drink, noticing fluorescent lights flicker once and air thicken with iron and burnt plastic.
output: The sink mirror is fogged; wiping condensation reveals slimy black hair oozing from the glass edges, dripping onto the sink. It wraps around the player’s wrists, ends sharp like broken glass, cutting through their shirt to draw blood. Pulling away, the faucet turns on by itself, lined with gnashing tiny teeth, spitting brown bloody water. The hair tightens, pulling their face toward the mirror—reflection is a crowd of distorted screaming library patrons. The teeth snap, water splashes onto hands, where blisters form and ooze, dissolving skin.

instruction: Describe a dark, labyrinthine sewer with slimy stone walls, stagnant water pooling underfoot, and no natural light.
input: You shine a small, dimming flashlight into the tunnel ahead to scan for a path.
output: The usual distant drips and faint pipe creaks of the sewer twist into gurgling whispers that seem to circle your flashlight beam, their tone too human for any drain. When the sound stops, the flashlight flickers and dies entirely, leaving you in a darkness so thick you can feel it pressing against your skin. The whispers’ echo lingers, as if they were waiting to take advantage of the sudden loss of light, unsettling and impossible to shake.

instruction: Describe a dark sewer with damp, muggy air and the faint reek of rot and sewage lining its narrow tunnels.
input: You reach for a rusted metal pipe jutting from the wall to steady your balance.
output: The second your gloved hand touches the pipe, the air around you plummets in temperature so fast your breath fogs immediately, like stepping into a deep freezer in the middle of summer. The fog clings to your tiny flashlight glow, blurring it until it’s almost gone, and a faint, cold whimper echoes from the tunnel’s far end—no sewer creature or pipe noise could produce that sharp, human sound. The cold seeps through your layers, turning your fingers numb, unsettlingly persistent in the total dark.

instruction: Describe a dark sewer cluttered with debris: broken bottles, rotting planks, and half-collapsed iron grates lining every corridor.
input: You step carefully over a jumble of sharp glass to move further down the tunnel.
output: Halfway across the debris pile, a heavy iron grate at the tunnel’s far end slides open on its own with a wet, grinding clank, as if pulled from the other side by an unseen force. The sound ripples through the stagnant water at your feet, making the rotting planks and broken bottles shift and slide even though there’s no wind or visible movement. Your flashlight catches the grate’s rusted edge, now glistening with something that isn’t just sewage, and a faint scratch comes from beyond the open gap.

instruction: Describe a dark sewer with curved stone walls and endless, identical passages that feel like they loop on themselves with no exit.
input: You turn your flashlight to follow a faint, echoing sound you just heard behind you.
output: When you turn, the tunnel you were standing in has transformed entirely: its stone walls now look like decaying, mottled flesh, the usual sewage reek replaced by a sharp, metallic smell of blood, and the drip of water is now the low, steady breathing of something large. The floor under your feet no longer feels like stone, but soft, yielding mud that sucks at your boots, and your flashlight’s beam stretches unnaturally far, vanishing into the dark as if the tunnel is swallowing the light whole. The echo you heard is now right next to your ear.

instruction: Describe a dark sewer with narrow tunnels, sticky mold on every surface, and a faint, distant hum in the walls.
input: You raise your flashlight to check behind a pile of moldy rags near the wall.
output: When you shine your light on the rags, they start to twitch and shift on their own, as if covered in tiny, unseen things, even though you see no visible movement. The shadow cast by your flashlight is not your own—it’s a taller, gaunt version of you with hollow eyes, leaning over the rags, and when you blink, it’s gone. The rags now smell like something rotting and alive, and the dark around you feels like it’s pressing closer, your tiny light suddenly too small to chase away the creeping dread.

instruction: A taxidermied great horned owl, disguised as a store security camera, that serves as the false security entity—its only purpose is to track and lure visitors into a suffocating embrace.
input: You browse the dusty taxidermy shop, running your fingers over a shelf of preserved songbird specimens, when you feel a chill and glance upward at the 'security camera' mounted above the register.
output: The owl’s head twists a full 270 degrees, its taxidermied glass eyes glistening with thin, opaque slime that drips onto the counter below. What looked like a lens is actually a slit in its beak, clicking softly as it 'focuses' on you. Its talons, sharp and coated in the same slime, extend from the mount, scraping against the wood to create a rhythm that matches your racing heartbeat. You try to move, but your feet are rooted— the slime has seeped through your shoe soles, sticking you to the floor. The owl’s hollow chest emits a low, purring hum that sounds exactly like your own voice, repeating your name over and over, as if it’s cataloging you for later.

instruction: A taxidermied German shepherd, marketed as a 'security deterrent' for the taxidermy shop, that lures victims back with distorted cries of help before attacking.
input: You hear a faint, human-sounding whimper coming from the back of the shop, and spot a hand-painted sign that reads 'SECURITY ALERT: BACK ROOM DANGER' next to the mounted German shepherd by the exit.
output: The shepherd’s tail twitches, a movement no taxidermied animal should make, and its jaws part with a wet, cracking sound—inside, instead of teeth, are rows of tiny, glowing insect eggs that pulse faintly. The whimper grows louder, and you realize it’s not coming from the back room, but from the shepherd itself: a high, girlish cry that warbles in time with its breathing. When you step toward it to investigate, its mounted paws lift, knocking over a jar of formaldehyde that splatters on your wrist, leaving a burning, metallic rash. The shepherd’s eyes lock on your arm, and it emits a low growl that mixes with the sound of your own skin crawling, as if it can taste the infection on your skin.

instruction: A taxidermied black cat labeled as 'security lock auxiliary' next to the shop’s display case keypads, that triggers a deadly trap when touched, mimicking human speech to lure victims.
input: You attempt to open a locked glass case containing a rare taxidermied raven, and notice a black cat taxidermy beside the keypad, blinking red lights and marked 'SECURITY AUX: DO NOT TOUCH' in peeling paint.
output: Your hand hesitates, then brushes the cat’s fur—rough, matted, and warm, like it’s still alive. Immediately, the red lights flash faster, and the cat’s mouth opens to speak in your childhood nanny’s voice: 'Come here, sweetie, let me help you.' A noxious mist, thick with the smell of burnt hair and rot, pours from its nostrils, stinging your eyes and making your lungs burn. The cat’s claws extend from its paws, sharp as needles, and it scratches at the glass case, leaving gouges that bleed a dark, tar-like fluid. You try to pull away, but the cat’s paws cling to your wrist, and its voice shifts to a growl, repeating 'Mine' over and over as the mist clouds your vision, making the shop’s taxidermied animals twist and stretch toward you.

instruction: A young defense lawyer is alone investigating a sealed exhibit locker in the nighttime courtroom after closing.
input: Player kneels and pries open the dusty metal locker marked with a faded name tag.
output: The locker holds a tattered child’s stuffed bear tucked under a stack of moldy case files, and a hidden compartment containing a key engraved with a vague serial number. At first, the bear feels like a sad trinket from an old custody case (a false lead, not tied to the current missing person probe), but a crumpled note behind the hinge reveals the key matches a storage unit number. The unit’s tenant was the only witness linking the defendant to a fatal accident—someone who has since vanished, left isolated to avoid testifying and silencing the truth.

instruction: A retired bailiff is alone in the empty nighttime courtroom, clearing desks after a small community event.
input: Player crouches behind the witness stand to grab a fallen pen.
output: They find a loose floorboard under the stand, pry it up, and discover two items: a crumpled note about cutting off all friends to hide a secret (a false lead, written by a teen who snuck in to write to their crush) and a metal box with a handcuff-shaped keychain. The note’s handwriting matches the judge’s late brother, who vanished after testifying against a local gang—hinting the gang isolated the brother to silence him, and the judge is now pressured into hiding the truth to protect his family.

instruction: A journalist is alone in the nighttime courthouse, reviewing sealed court documents for a missing person story.
input: Player unlocks a locked file cabinet labeled ‘unsolved 1998 cases’ with a provided code.
output: Inside, they find a case file for a missing court clerk, alongside a fake obituary (a false lead, misprinted from the local paper’s error section) and redacted witness statements. The only unredacted line reads: ‘all witnesses have been isolated, no one speaks of her disappearance.’ A faint scratch on the file edge matches handcuff marks, hinting the clerk wasn’t just lost—she was taken, kept alone somewhere to prevent exposing a corruption scheme tied to courthouse leadership.

instruction: A new court clerk is alone in the nighttime courtroom, organizing evidence shelves after a weekend training session.
input: Player pulls a dusty box labeled ‘probate cases 2015-2020’ from the top shelf.
output: The box holds a handwritten journal from a deceased probate judge, where he writes about professional friction with a local attorney (a false lead, his ‘ostracism’ was just minor professional pushback). But his last entry has a note to find a hidden safe in the judge’s quarters, with a line: ‘they left me alone to fix this, but the safe has proof.’ The hint is clear: the attorney isolated the judge to keep him from exposing shady will transactions, and the clerk now holds the clue to uncover the crime.

instruction: A forensic artist is alone in the nighttime courtroom, storing old case sketches in the evidence room.
input: Player searches a crate marked ‘unidentified suspects’ for a cold case sketch.
output: Inside the crate, they find a sketch of a man with a scar across his left cheek (a false lead, it’s a duplicate of a recent art contest sketch left by mistake) and a folded note in faint pencil. The note says: ‘the witness who saw him is alone in a downtown clinic, too scared to talk.’ This hints the scarred man is a suspect in an unsolved assault, who isolated the witness to prevent identification—setting up a future threat that could escalate if the witness is left unprotected.

instruction: A dim, decaying asylum hallway at 1:47AM, peeling floral wallpaper, half-broken linoleum tiles, fluorescent lights buzzing so loudly your teeth ache
input: You step off the warped tile to avoid the sharp creak it makes under pressure
output: As your weight shifts, the distant sobs you thought came from the end of the hall cut off abruptly. Instead, a slow scuff of a shoe sounds behind you—too close, too familiar, like the drag of a straightjacket’s fabric. When you spin, the hallway is empty, but the sobs resume louder, right at your shoulder, even though no one is within arm’s reach. Your hands shake, and you can’t tell if it’s from the chill or the rising paranoia that someone is hiding just out of sight, waiting to corner you in this endless dark hall

instruction: Same asylum hallway at 1:52AM, fog creeping under cell doors, your breath visible in the faint fluorescent glow
input: You reach for the nearest cell door’s cold brass doorknob to peek inside
output: The moment your fingers wrap around the metal, the temperature drops sharply—your breath turns to a thick cloud that lingers, and fog curls up your ankles faster than before. The cell’s window fogs over completely, and you swear you see a pale handprint press from the inside, even though you passed this cell minutes ago and it was empty. The cold seeps into your bones, and your teeth chatter, but the room isn’t actually that cold; your mind races, convinced the asylum is freezing you out, trapping you in this cold, endless hallway forever

instruction: Asylum hallway at 2:01AM, a rusted medicine cart parked mid-hall, its wheels sticking on cracked linoleum
input: You grab the cart’s metal handle to move it out of your path
output: When your hand closes around the handle, the cart lurches backward on its own, dragging the wheels with a harsh, scraping sound. You yank your hand away immediately, heart pounding, and the cart stops—right back where it started, like it never moved. The label on a nearby pill bottle falls off, drifting to your feet, and it reads: “For the paranoid ones.” The cart’s tiny drawer pops open, a syringe rolling out toward your shoe, even though you saw all drawers locked when you passed earlier

instruction: Asylum hallway at 2:07AM, faded drawings of former patients line the walls, one depicting a hallway identical to this one
input: You reach to touch the faded drawing on the wall next to cell 17
output: As your fingers brush the paper, the hallway around you warps. The walls stretch, making the hall twice as long as it was, and the fluorescent lights stop buzzing entirely, leaving only the faint glow from the drawing. The patients in the drawing begin to walk, their hollow eyes fixed on you. You blink, and the hallway snaps back to normal, but your hand now has a faint, matching smudge of faded ink on it—even though you didn’t touch the drawing hard enough to leave a mark. You panic, realizing the asylum’s reality is unraveling around you, trapping you in its looping, twisted space

instruction: Asylum hallway at 2:12AM, the distant clock tower chimes faintly through the walls, echoing down the empty hall
input: You cup your hands and call out, “Is anyone here?” to test for hidden occupants
output: Your voice echoes down the hall, then fades, but a hollow, mocking reply comes back: “No one here but you.” It sounds exactly like your own voice, just deeper and rougher, stripped of warmth. The clock tower chimes again, but it’s off by one—four chimes instead of three, making your chest tight. You spin wildly, expecting to see someone, but the hall remains empty. A chorus of lock clicks sounds from every cell at once, and you realize the clock is the only thing anchoring you here, and it’s breaking, dragging you further into the asylum’s paranoia-fueled void

instruction: You are stationed in a submarine’s navigation nook, where the steady hum of the fusion reactor and distant water pressure thrum is the only constant during a routine deep-sea patrol. A faint, unidentifiable clicking starts just above the background noise, not logged in the ship’s system.
input: You reach for the panel to disable the noise-canceling function to hear more clearly.
output: When you toggle the noise canceler off, the clicking swells into the sound of someone typing on a keyboard—slow, deliberate, from the empty storage locker behind you. You spin, but the locker door is latched tight, no gaps for a hand, yet the typing continues, matching the rhythm of the controls you just touched, as if it’s copying every input you made, even though no one else is on this watch shift.

instruction: You’re in the submarine’s galley, where the water heater’s low hum keeps the air warm, and the smell of stale coffee lingers from an hour ago. The interior temperature gauge reads a steady 68 degrees Fahrenheit, as it has for days.
input: You reach for the coffee pot to pour a cup for your shift partner.
output: When you wrap your hand around the coffee pot’s handle, the metal is ice cold, despite the gauge’s reading. The galley’s temperature drops 10 degrees in three seconds, the walls sweating condensation that freezes on the metal counter. The coffee pot’s label melts off, leaving a blank, smooth surface, and you hear your shift partner’s voice say, “Who turned down the heat?” behind you, even though they’re on the aft deck.

instruction: You’re in the submarine’s torpedo bay, where all ordnance is secured in locked brackets, and dim red light casts sharp shadows on the steel walls. The bay has been locked from the bridge for 48 hours, no one has entered.
input: You reach for the bracketed torpedo to check its alignment, per the maintenance checklist.
output: When your hand touches the torpedo’s casing, it shifts half an inch in its bracket, like it’s just moved on its own. The bracket’s lock clicks open, even though the bridge hasn’t sent a release code. You pull back, and the torpedo’s nose tilts toward you, not as a weapon, but as if it’s watching you, its metal surface reflecting your face slightly off-kilter, like a cracked mirror.

instruction: You’re in the submarine’s bridge, where the sonar screen displays only the usual empty deep sea, the depth gauge holding steady at 12,000 feet, no surface ships nearby. The intercom crackles once, as it does during minor pressure shifts.
input: You lean closer to the sonar screen to adjust the gain, trying to make out faint rock formations below.
output: When you adjust the gain, the sonar screen clears to show a bright, sunlit ocean surface, complete with seagulls flying overhead—even though the depth gauge still says 12,000 feet, and the bridge’s portholes are sealed tight against deep-sea pressure. The intercom plays the sound of crashing waves and wind, like the submarine is suddenly on the surface, even though your suit’s pressure gauge still reads 12,000 feet of water.

instruction: You’re at the submarine’s periscope, the lens fogged slightly from recent pressure shifts, the view set to scan for surface contacts during your watch. The periscope’s controls stick slightly when you turn it, as they have for days.
input: You twist the periscope’s adjustment knob to zoom in on a faint blip on the surface map.
output: When you twist the knob, the periscope unfolds to full length, sticking out of the submarine’s conning tower, even though the depth gauge remains at 10,000 feet. The view clears to show a busy harbor full of lit civilian ships, like it’s mid-day, even though the periscope was set to scan open ocean. A crew member’s voice says, “We’re here?” from behind you, even though no one else is on watch, and the harbor’s lights flicker like failing power, even though the submarine’s power is at full.

instruction: A tired night shift nurse in a faded grey uniform, holding a dented metal flashlight that flickers erratically, leans against a cinder block wall in the asylum’s dim hallway.
input: Is there a way out of this hallway?
output: The nurse’s sputtering flashlight gurgles once, casting a wavering, tiny circle of light that only reaches three feet ahead. Her voice is hoarse at first, almost gentle as she answers, “Exit’s at the end… but don’t wander past the light.” When the light flickers out for two heartbeats, her next word is a sharp, panicked whisper: “You can’t see it, but the dark’s breathing. It’s right behind you, and it doesn’t like people who leave the light.” The light flares back abruptly, and you spot her hands are translucent at the edges, like the dark is slowly eating her from the fingertips up.

instruction: A shivering asylum patient in a wrinkled white gown, rocking back and forth by a locked door with a fogged window, eyes darting constantly between the hallway and the dark spaces under adjacent doors.
input: What are you doing here?
output: The patient stops rocking abruptly, head jerking up like they just heard something only the dark could hear. At first, they mumble softly, “Waiting… for the light to come back.” Their voice climbs an octave, turning frantic, when a shadow slips from under a far door: “It took the last one. The dark doesn’t like when people look away. Don’t turn your back, don’t let the dark touch you—” They cut off screaming as something yanks their arm into the dark under the door, their gown vanishing inch by inch until only their wide, terrified eyes stare from the edge of the remaining light.

instruction: A gnarled older groundskeeper in oilskin overalls, holding a rusted skeleton key, stands at the end of the dim hallway, his face half swallowed by shadow, boots scuffing the linoleum.
input: Do you work here? Can you help me?
output: The groundskeeper takes a slow step forward, his key clinking softly in his weathered hand. His voice is rough, like he’s spent decades talking to empty walls: “Work here? I hold the keys to all the dark rooms. The ones the light never reaches.” When he notices your gaze flick to his hands, his smile twists—his right hand is half dissolved, the dark from the hallway bleeding into his skin like spilled ink. “You shouldn’t look away. The dark takes what it can see. You’ve got a light? Good. Don’t let it go out. I tried, once… it got me.”

instruction: A teenaged orderly in a crisp white uniform, holding a crumpled clipboard, walks fast down the hallway, his shoes silent on the scuffed linoleum, face initially smooth and earnest.
input: Wait, can you tell me if someone’s looking for me?
output: The orderly skids to a stop, turning to face you, but his face twists before he speaks—his mouth gapes wider than a human jaw should, no movement of his facial muscles, like wax melting into shadow. His voice is high and wrong, pitched too thin: “Looking for you? The dark is. It’s been watching you since you turned the corner.” His clipboard slips from his grip, and when it hits the floor, the papers flutter into the dark beyond the flashlight’s reach, vanishing before they touch the ground. “The light can’t hide you forever. It’s hungry for what it can’t see, and what it can.”

instruction: An emaciated former asylum doctor in a stained lab coat, holding a glowing blue vial, stands under a single flickering overhead light, his bones visible through thin fabric.
input: What is that vial you’re holding?
output: The doctor’s sunken eyes focus first on the vial, his voice calm at first, like explaining a forgotten medical procedure: “This keeps the dark away. The light is just its thin cover.” He lifts the vial higher, and the overhead light flickers out for two seconds, revealing inky dark seeping through hallway cracks, pooling around his feet like living smoke. “It feeds on what it can’t see. You have a light? Great. Don’t let it run out. I did, once… it drank me from the inside out.” His hand shakes, the vial’s glow dimming, making the dark rush closer to your feet.

instruction: The station’s only working interface is a dimly lit console labeled 'LIFE SUPPORT PRIORITY' near the core.
input: Player leans in, scanning the console’s access pad to see if they can pull up system details.
output: The console first flashes a false security alert: a pop-up warning of a minor coolant leak in Module B, marked 'LOW RISK' with a button to 'Acknowledge & Reset'—a classic distraction. But when the player ignores the alert to dig deeper, a hidden log snippet under the surface reveals the station’s primary defense grid was disabled by a corrupted protocol three months prior. The note ends with 'We thought it was a false alarm, so no one fixed it. Now we’re trapped.'

instruction: A dented storage closet near the airlock is labeled 'SECURITY DEPOT' with a partially stuck lock.
input: Player uses their multitool to pick the lock, expecting emergency survival gear.
output: Inside are empty racks marked 'EMERGENCY LIFE RAFTS'—the compartments hold only outdated, cracked foam padding, a false lead that makes the depot seem abandoned and safe. Tucked behind a loose shelf, however, is a scratched access card and a note: 'Don’t trust the external comms—they’re linked to the station’s destruction trigger. We hid this because command said we were overreacting to the security breaches.'

instruction: The station’s intercom suddenly crackles to life with a static-laced pre-recorded message.
input: Player turns up the volume to parse the audio, thinking it might have useful emergency guidance.
output: The first part is a standard, 11-year-old safety announcement reminding crew to check oxygen canisters—a false comfort, like the station’s old security system that was once deemed reliable but is now useless. Halfway through, the audio cuts to a distorted whisper: 'They tampered with the auto-locks. We warned command, but they said we were making false security claims.' Static blares again, and the player spots a scratch on the intercom edge, proof the second track was hidden.

instruction: A corroded wall panel labeled 'ALERT TEST SYSTEM' is the only unrusted hardware near the docking bay.
input: Player pries open the panel to inspect its wiring and any remaining internal components.
output: The panel’s interior is cluttered with dead test batteries and a faded sign reading 'ALL DRILLS PASSED' from 12 years prior—this false security marker makes the station seem to have a history of reliable safety protocols. Tucked behind frayed wiring, though, is a half-buried data chip. Its first line reads: 'We disabled the auto-seal in Section 3—command said it was a false security risk, but it’s failing. Two life signs trigger a lock that kills the crew.'

instruction: A small terminal sits on a desk by the command core, its screen glowing with a persistent green 'SECURE' banner.
input: Player fumbles with the terminal’s power button to boot it up, hoping for mission logs or escape coordinates.
output: The boot sequence loads a fake, polished mission briefing: the station was decommissioned for planned upgrades, all security protocols were active and functional—false security reassurance to lull anyone exploring into complacency. After waiting 30 seconds, the screen flickers and a hidden log pops up: the 'SECURE' banner is a hack. The terminal faked security status while the reactor leaked radiation, and the crew was ordered to hide the truth as a false alarm.

instruction: Write a horror scene
input: Second-person, body horror, setting: after hours public library, books merging with your skin
output: You linger after curfew to return a vintage poetry collection, the hallway smelling like mildew and old paper. When you reach for the 1923 shelf, its rigid spine catches your forearm, slicing through your flannel. Before you can yank back, thin pages fuse to your wound, seeping into your pores. You watch as words scrawl under your skin—‘The Woods’—spreading up your wrist. Your fingers tingle, and when you clench them, they crinkle like folded parchment, tips turning yellowed, brittle. The library’s faint hum swells; more spines twist toward you, hungry to bind more flesh to their stories.

instruction: Generate a horror scene
input: Second-person, body horror, setting: after hours public library, library cards multiplying from your fingers
output: You restock the graphic novel section after closing, when your thumb brushes the check-out desk slot. A sharp pinch, and a thin plastic library card sprouts from your fingertip, emblazoned with a stranger’s name: ‘Mara Hale, 1952’. You yank it off, but another bursts from your index, then dozens more—each with a new stranger’s name, edges digging like shards of plastic. Your skin itches, cards multiplying faster, wrapping your wrist like a choking bracelet, their numbers glowing faint blue as they fuse to your blood vessels. The library’s lights flicker, and a whisper of old names curls around you.

instruction: Write a horror scene
input: Second-person, body horror, setting: after hours public library, Dewey decimal numbers rearranging your bones
output: You hide in the reference section after hours, flipping a history book. When you run your finger over its Dewey sticker (940.1), a jolt shoots up your arm, and your ribs ache—they shift, spacing exactly 9.4 cm apart, matching the number. You gasp, your spine curving to align with the decimal’s second digit, 0.1. Your fingers bend, joints positioned like the sticker’s printed lines. When you stand, your knees click like book hinges, legs folding into a shelf bracket’s shape. The numbers on every nearby book glow, digits crawling toward your skin, ready to rearrange all your bones to their order.

instruction: Create a horror scene
input: Second-person, body horror, setting: after hours public library, dust bunnies burrowing into your throat
output: You cough in the silent stacks, dust coating your lungs. A tiny squirming dust bunny slides into your mouth, and you swallow it without thinking. Within seconds, your throat burns like swallowing rotten wet paper. You touch your neck, skin feeling soft, malleable like an old paperback cover. When you clear your throat, a strip of your tongue peels off, thin as a page, and flutters to the floor. More dust bunnies multiply, crawling into your ears and nose, feeding on your flesh’s glue. Your cheeks sag, turning yellowed and brittle, like a book left in rain, each breath scraping paper-thin tissue.

instruction: Generate a horror scene
input: Second-person, body horror, setting: after hours public library, overdue dates burning into your wrist
output: You find a locked basement nook, a shelf holding books with red overdue stamps dating to 1927. Curious, you pull out a leather-bound novel, and its embossed date burns into your wrist—1927, sharp as a stamp, searing through your skin. You yank back, staring as the date swells into a raised pattern, like the book’s spine itself. Flexing your hand, your palm feels rough, covered in the book’s cloth, tiny gold letters forming: *Tales of the Forgotten*. Your fingertips harden, nails turning into spine ridges, and you hear faint page rustle as your skin shifts, ready to make you another volume the library never lets go.

instruction: A haggard nurse in a starched white uniform sits on a scuffed hallway bench, flipping a tattered medical journal as if it’s a new book, her posture stiff, eyes hidden behind thin wire-rim glasses
input: Do you know where Dr. Hale's office is?
output: The nurse doesn’t look up at first, her thumb rubbing the same page edge over and over until the paper frays into ragged strips that flutter to the floor with a soft rustle. When she finally lifts her head, her eyes are too bright, too clear—like cracked glass glued back together wrong. She taps the journal’s cover, and you notice the name "Patient 7" scrawled across the spine in ink that matches the frayed pages. "Dr. Hale? He’s right here," she says. "He’s been waiting for you to read his favorite story. The one about people who forget how to leave hallways. Don’t worry, he’s very patient."

instruction: A silent patient in faded gray pajamas leans against a hallway wall, holding a chipped teacup that seems to steam even though the hallway’s air is cold enough to make your breath fog
input: Is it cold in here, or is it just me?
output: The patient blinks slow, like they just realized you’re standing there. They take a small sip of tea, then hold the cup out to you, steam curling up to fog the space between you. "Cold? No, the walls are warm," they say, tapping the brick behind them. "They keep all the names safe. I wrote mine on them—three times. Don’t you remember your name? It’s on the floor tiles too, look." They shift their slippered foot, and you see grout lines under the tiles scrawled with tiny, blurry letters that slip away when you try to focus. "If you forget, the walls will hold you too. Like they held the last one."

instruction: A rumpled orderly in a stained blue uniform pushes a heavy linen cart down the hallway, humming an off-key tune that seems to vibrate through the linoleum tiles
input: Can I help you with that cart?
output: The orderly stops abruptly, their humming cutting off so sharply it makes your ears ring. They glance at the cart, then at you, their face neutral until a grin spreads that doesn’t reach their eyes, like a mask stretching too thin. "Help? No need. These are for the ones who talk too much," they say, yanking back a corner of the linen sheet draped over the top. You glimpse a tuft of hair—brown, the same color as yours—peeking out. "You talk too much too, don’t you? The walls told me. They said to get extra linen for you. You’ll fit right in here." The grin widens, and you notice their ears are pointed, like they’re listening to something only the hallway walls can hear.

instruction: An elderly woman sits cross-legged on the hallway floor, braiding a long black thread that stretches from her hands all the way to the next open doorway, her hands moving with frantic, steady speed
input: What are you doing back here?
output: The woman doesn’t look up, her fingers working the thread as if it’s alive. "Braiding the missing," she says, quiet, like she’s talking to the thread itself. "They left their pieces behind—eyes, fingers, names. I braid them so they don’t get lost." She lifts her hand, and you spot a tiny gray finger bone tied into the thread with an impossibly neat knot. "You left your name by the bench," she says, finally glancing up, her eyes milky like fogged glass. "I’m adding it next. It will make the braid stronger. You’ll like being part of it."

instruction: A young man stands in the open doorway of a room marked "Empty", holding a small hand mirror that only reflects the empty hallway behind him, not his face
input: Is this room safe?
output: The man turns his head slow, like his neck is stuck in place. The mirror doesn’t move, still showing the same empty hallway. "Safe? It’s the only place that’s safe," he says, stepping out of the doorway—his feet making no sound on the linoleum. "All the others remember. All the others leave. This one remembers to stay. Just like you should." He holds the mirror out to you, and when you glance in, your reflection’s face is the same milky gray as his, no features, no expression. "You forgot to look in the mirror when you came in. I had to remind you. Don’t worry, I fixed it. You’ll fit right in with the ones who don’t need faces."
